meta_description: Editorial strategy, structural and analytical editing, copyediting and style guides, and production and proofing. What each involves and how I decide what to change.
hero_heading: Every edit should earn its place
hero_lead: Before I change anything, I'm asking one of three questions: is this necessary, because something is wrong. Is this worth it, because something would genuinely work better. Or is this just a preference, another perfectly good way to write the same sentence. I mostly leave the third category alone. Not every editor does, and I think that's the difference between an edit that helps and one that just proves someone was paid to make changes.

sections.heading: Strategy & direction
sections.body: Before anyone writes a word, someone has to decide what a publication or content program is actually for, who it serves, and why it should exist in its current form. I've taken a magazine through a full strategic reset: length, frequency, tone, and the editorial rationale that has to justify every commission. I plan in cycles that match how a business actually operates, budget season, results season, planning season, rather than chasing a news calendar that doesn't belong to the organisation.
items: Commissioning and managing a bench of writers and contributors
Interview design and executive access, including chief executive and board-level interviews

sections.heading: Structure & substance
sections.body: This is where most of the value in editing actually lives, and where most people don't think to look. I take pages of scattered, high-conviction thinking, often from someone senior with strong instincts and no time for the mechanics of argument, and find the version that's both accurate and worth reading. I look for the point a writer has already made but buried in paragraph four, and give it the position it deserves.
items: Ghostwriting for senior leaders and subject-matter experts
Analytical editing: checking that a document's conclusions are as strong as its evidence
Identifying where an argument is more definitive, or less, than the evidence actually supports

sections.heading: Clarity, consistency & style
sections.body: At sentence level, I edit for clarity, rhythm and precision without stripping out the voice that made something worth reading in the first place. At document level, I check the things readers never notice unless they're wrong: whether the number in the executive summary matches the number in table four, whether an acronym is defined on first use, whether 270 km and 270 kilometres are both sitting in the same report. I build style guides from how an organisation actually publishes, not from a theoretical document nobody consults.
items: Technical and specialist editing: I query rather than rewrite when I'm out of my depth
Style guide development and gap analysis
Cross-document consistency checks: numbers, dates, terminology, units, acronyms

sections.heading: Production & proof
sections.body: I stay involved once a story moves into layout. I can go into InDesign myself to reflow copy and fix a page that's already been signed off, under deadline, when a contributor's final draft lands late. At final proof, restraint matters as much as attention. The closer a document gets to publication, the more dangerous an unnecessary change becomes.

closing_heading: Not sure which of this you need?
closing_body: That's a completely normal place to start. Tell me what the document or publication is meant to do, and we'll work out the rest from there.
